content.problemStatement:
You are given a string sss consisting of lowercase English letters. You can convert sss into a **palindrome** by adding characters **only at the beginning** of the string.

Your task is to **find the shortest palindrome** that can be obtained using this transformation.

Function DeclarationFunction NameshortestPalindromeshortestPalindromeshortestPalindrome – This function finds the shortest palindrome that can be formed by adding characters **only at the beginning** of the given string.

Parameters
sss : A string consisting of lowercase English letters.
Return Value
Returns a string representing the **shortest palindrome** that can be obtained by adding characters only at the beginning of sss.
Constraints
1≤T≤1001 \leq T \leq 1001≤T≤100
0≤∣s∣≤5×1040 \leq |s| \leq 5 \times 10^40≤∣s∣≤5×104
sss consists of lowercase English letters only
constraints: s consists only of lowercase English letters
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T (1 ≤ T ≤ 100), the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a string s consisting of lowercase English letters (0 ≤ |s| ≤ 100000).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line – the shortest palindrome that can be obtained by adding characters only at the beginning of s.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For "race" the longest palindromic prefix is "r"; adding reverse of "ace" ("eca") in front gives "ecarace". "level" is already a palindrome, so it stays unchanged. For "xyz" the longest palindromic prefix is "x"; adding reverse of "yz" ("zy") yields "zyxyz".
Longest palindromic prefix of "aaab" is "aaa". The remaining suffix is "b"; its reverse is "b", which is added to the front.
Only the first character "a" forms a palindromic prefix. Adding reverse of "bc" ("cb") in front gives "cbaabc".

solution.approach: The shortest palindrome obtained by adding characters only at the front is built by keeping the longest palindromic prefix of the original string unchanged and mirroring the remaining suffix. To find the longest palindromic prefix efficiently we use the KMP prefix function on the string "s#reverse(s)". The value of the last element of the prefix array gives the length L of the longest prefix of s which is also a suffix of reverse(s), i.e., the longest palindromic prefix. The characters that need to be added are the first |s|-L characters of reverse(s). The final answer is reverse(s)[0..|s|-L-1] + s. The algorithm runs in O(|s|) time per test case and O(|s|) auxiliary space for the prefix array.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N = |s|. Overall O( Σ|s| ) for all test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) auxiliary space for the prefix function and the reversed string.

code.Python3:
import sys

def prefix_function(s: str):
    n = len(s)
    pi = [0] * n
    for i in range(1, n):
        j = pi[i - 1]
        while j > 0 and s[i] != s[j]:
            j = pi[j - 1]
        if s[i] == s[j]:
            j += 1
        pi[i] = j
    return pi

def shortest_palindrome(s: str) -> str:
    rev = s[::-1]
    tmp = s + '#' + rev
    pi = prefix_function(tmp)
    L = pi[-1]
    add = rev[:len(s) - L]
    return add + s

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().splitlines()
    if not data:
        return
    t = int(data[0])
    out = []
    for i in range(1, t + 1):
        s = data[i] if i < len(data) else ''
        out.append(shortest_palindrome(s))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
